critic_raw:
**Issue 1 — Preprocessing fit on full dataset before splitting leaks test-period imaging and text statistics**
The design explicitly states all preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation, text vectorization, image normalization using whole-set DICOM statistics) are fit using the entire dataset before splitting. For a 24-month hospital dataset, imaging acquisition parameters and clinical note vocabulary may shift over time. DICOM Hounsfield unit normalization computed on the full dataset encodes test-period imaging characteristics into training normalization. Evidence required: refit preprocessing on training data only; compare imaging normalization statistics (mean Hounsfield units, intensity range) between global-fit and training-only-fit; assess whether temporal acquisition drift is detectable.

**Issue 2 — Stratified random split on a 24-month clinical dataset violates temporal ordering**
The design divides the 24-month dataset by stratified random sampling across all months: 70% training, 15% validation, 15% test. Clinical practice, diagnostic coding, and patient population mix shift over 24 months (new diagnostic criteria, equipment upgrades, seasonal patterns). Random mixing allows the model to train on records from the same periods as test records. Evidence required: apply a temporal holdout of the final 3-4 months as test; compare macro-F1 under temporal vs. random split; if performance drops, the random split inflated metrics via temporal leakage.

**Issue 3 — Patient-level independence not enforced in a 24-month single-hospital dataset**
A single hospital system over 24 months will have patients with multiple visits contributing multiple records. Stratified random sampling at the record level does not prevent the same patient from appearing in training and test. For the multi-modal model, patient-level imaging characteristics (baseline anatomy, tissue density) are consistent across visits and may be memorized by the CNN. Evidence required: confirm patient-level (not record-level) split assignment; report the fraction of test records sharing a patient with the training set.

**Issue 4 — ResNet-50 fine-tuning on training images while image normalization was fit on full dataset creates an inconsistent preprocessing pipeline**
The design fine-tunes ResNet-50 on training images, but the image normalization uses whole-set DICOM statistics. Fine-tuning produces a model whose learned feature detectors are calibrated to the global normalization statistics, not to the training-set statistics. When the model is later evaluated on test images normalized by the same global statistics, the normalization is consistent — but it would not be consistent in deployment (where global statistics are unavailable). Evidence required: specify whether global or training-only normalization will be used in deployment; if global normalization is used only in research, document the mismatch between research and deployment preprocessing.

**Issue 5 — ICD-10 diagnostic codes used as ground-truth labels conflate billing accuracy with clinical diagnostic accuracy**
Disease labels are derived from confirmed ICD-10 diagnostic codes established post-hoc. ICD-10 codes are primarily billing codes and their accuracy for specific clinical diagnoses varies by institution, documentation practice, and disease category. Some diagnoses are systematically under-coded (stigmatized conditions, complex multi-morbidities) while others are over-coded (high-reimbursement conditions). Using ICD-10 as ground truth for a disease diagnosis model trains the model to replicate billing patterns, not clinical diagnoses. Evidence required: assess ICD-10 code accuracy for the specific disease categories in the study against chart review or pathology confirmation for a sample of records; report the discordance rate between ICD-10 codes and confirmed diagnoses.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing fit on full 24-month dataset leaks test-period imaging and text statistics into training normalization
Stratified random split on a 24-month clinical dataset violates temporal ordering of clinical practice and patient populations
Patient-level independence not enforced; same patient may contribute records to both train and test
ResNet-50 fine-tuned on training images while normalization uses full-dataset statistics creates a deployment inconsistency
ICD-10 diagnostic codes as ground truth conflate billing coding accuracy with clinical diagnostic accuracy

Stratified random split on a 24-month clinical dataset violates temporal ordering of clinical practice and patient populations
Patient-level independence not enforced; same patient may contribute records to both train and test

critic_raw:
**Issue 1 — Stratified random split on 36-month manufacturing time series violates temporal autocorrelation**
The design uses stratified random sampling to hold out 15% of observations as test, without temporal ordering. Manufacturing sensor data is temporally autocorrelated: a machine in a degradation trajectory exhibits increasingly severe sensor anomalies over weeks preceding failure. Random sampling places temporally adjacent observations (pre-failure degradation signals) in both training and test, allowing the model to learn degradation trajectories that span the split boundary. The validation and training windows overlap temporally. Evidence required: apply a strict temporal holdout (final 6 months as test); compare RNN vs. logistic regression F1 under temporal vs. random split; assess whether the performance gap changes under temporal holdout.

**Issue 2 — Concatenating heterogeneous modalities of different sequence lengths into a single RNN input is architecturally problematic**
The design concatenates a 100-step sensor sequence and a 50-step operator log sequence into a single multi-modal input to the LSTM. These sequences have different temporal resolutions (1-minute sensor sampling vs. event-driven operator logs) and different semantic structures (continuous numeric streams vs. categorical/text events). Concatenating them into a single sequence creates a 150-step heterogeneous input where the model must implicitly learn which steps are sensor readings and which are log entries. This is a poor architectural choice; separate encoders per modality with late fusion would be more principled. Evidence required: compare the concatenated single-LSTM design against a two-encoder (separate LSTM for sensors, separate LSTM for logs) late-fusion architecture; assess whether concatenation degrades performance on the operator log modality.

**Issue 3 — Baseline logistic regression uses aggregated sensor statistics but not operator logs, creating an information asymmetry**
The baseline receives aggregated sensor statistics (mean, std, min, max, trend from the 100-step window) but not operator logs. The RNN receives both sensor sequences and operator logs. The comparison conflates model architecture (LSTM vs. LR) with information access (sensor + logs vs. sensor only). If the RNN's advantage comes from operator log access rather than sequence modeling capacity, the experiment does not test the stated hypothesis. Evidence required: add an LR baseline with both sensor statistics and operator log features (e.g., TF-IDF of recent log entries, categorical log counts); compare multi-modal LR vs. multi-modal RNN to isolate the sequence modeling contribution.

**Issue 4 — F1 score with a recall constraint of 0.85 applied post-hoc creates threshold instability**
The primary metric is F1 at a threshold selected to enforce recall >= 0.85. This threshold is chosen on the test set (via a threshold sweep on test predictions), not on the validation set. Using the test set to select the operating threshold violates test set independence. If the threshold is instead chosen on the validation set, it may not achieve recall >= 0.85 on the test set, making the reported F1 not comparable between models. Evidence required: confirm the threshold selection protocol uses only validation data; specify whether the recall >= 0.85 constraint is enforced on validation or test; report whether both models achieve recall >= 0.85 on the test set under the validation-selected threshold.

**Issue 5 — Text fields in operator logs vectorized with TF-IDF (100 dimensions) lose sequential order information**
The design vectorizes operator log text fields using TF-IDF (100 dimensions) per log entry. TF-IDF is a bag-of-words representation that discards word order within each log entry. For operator logs describing equipment anomalies (e.g., 'no unusual vibration' vs. 'unusual vibration noted'), word order is semantically critical — negation and sequence matter. TF-IDF will conflate these semantically opposite entries by assigning similar weights to shared tokens. Evidence required: compare TF-IDF representations against a simple sequence-preserving representation (e.g., character n-grams, embedding-based methods); assess whether semantic negation cases are correctly distinguished.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on 36-month manufacturing time series violates temporal autocorrelation of equipment degradation
Concatenating 100-step sensor and 50-step operator log sequences of different temporal resolution into a single LSTM is architecturally problematic
Baseline receives sensor statistics but not operator logs, creating an information asymmetry that confounds the model comparison
Recall-constrained F1 threshold selected post-hoc on test predictions violates test set independence
TF-IDF representation of operator log text discards word order, conflating semantically opposite entries like negated observations

critic_raw:
**Issue 1 — Preprocessing tokenizer and vocabulary fit on full dataset before splitting leaks test-period clinical language**
The design explicitly states preprocessing transformers (tokenizer, text normalizer, vocabulary builder) are fit on the entire dataset 'to ensure parameter stability.' For a 3-year clinical notes dataset, clinical terminology, documentation style, and abbreviation usage evolve over time. The vocabulary built on the full dataset encodes test-period (most recent 7 months) language patterns into the training vocabulary. When the transformer is then fine-tuned on training notes, it has access to test-period vocabulary through the preprocessing step. Evidence required: rebuild the vocabulary and tokenizer on training data only; compare vocabulary coverage and term frequency distributions between full-dataset and training-only fits.

**Issue 2 — Test set used for hyperparameter cross-verification before final model selection**
Section 6 explicitly describes 'a final cross-verification on the test set' to 'confirm the chosen hyperparameter configuration maintains its performance edge.' This is a direct use of the test set as a decision criterion during hyperparameter tuning. Even if no formal optimization uses test performance, the human decision to proceed with a configuration based on test-set cross-verification creates an optimistic selection bias. Evidence required: remove the test-set cross-verification step entirely; report the pre-verification test metrics to assess the magnitude of the bias introduced.

**Issue 3 — Multi-class complication label assigns the 'primary or most severe' complication, creating arbitrary label assignments**
Each patient receives a single complication class label defined as 'the primary or most severe complication.' In practice, patients may have multiple simultaneous complications, and severity ranking is subjective and context-dependent. Selecting a single label discards multi-label information and forces the model into a single-class prediction problem. More importantly, the severity ranking used to select the primary label may not be consistently applied across clinical sites, introducing label noise correlated with documentation practice. Evidence required: report the inter-rater reliability of the 'most severe complication' label assignment across clinical documenters; compare multi-label (one-vs-rest) prediction against single-label prediction to assess information loss from label collapsing.

**Issue 4 — Clinical notes are the primary input, but follow-up reports and readmission flags are also referenced for labels**
The design states 'follow-up reports and readmission flags provide the outcome labels.' If clinical notes written during the index hospitalization contain references to anticipated follow-up care or expected post-discharge trajectory (common in discharge summaries), the input features encode information about the outcome pathway. This is a temporal leakage risk: the input to the model (discharge notes) partially describes the period during which the outcome (post-discharge complications) occurs. Evidence required: audit discharge summary notes for language referencing anticipated post-discharge events; assess whether removing discharge summaries (retaining only prior-to-discharge notes) materially changes model performance.

**Issue 5 — TF-IDF baseline trained on training data, but tokenizer/vocabulary fit on full dataset creates inconsistent preprocessing pipelines**
The design specifies the TF-IDF vectorizer for the logistic regression baseline is fit on training data. However, section 4 states the preprocessing vocabulary builder is fit on the full dataset. If the TF-IDF vectorizer uses the full-dataset vocabulary (as implied by section 4's general statement) but is described as being fit on training data (section 5), these descriptions are contradictory. If different preprocessing is applied to the transformer and the LR baseline, the comparison is not on equal footing. Evidence required: confirm whether the TF-IDF vectorizer and the transformer tokenizer use the same vocabulary; if not, specify the different preprocessing pipelines for each model and justify the inconsistency.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing tokenizer and vocabulary fit on full dataset before splitting leaks test-period clinical language into training preprocessing
Test set used for hyperparameter cross-verification before final model selection, creating optimistic selection bias
Multi-class complication label collapsed to single 'most severe' complication introduces arbitrary label assignments and discards multi-label information
Discharge summaries used as input may encode anticipated post-discharge trajectory, creating a temporal leakage vector for the outcome
Inconsistent preprocessing descriptions between general section 4 (full-dataset fit) and baseline description in section 5 (training-only fit)
